critic_raw:
The methodology is well-structured and thoughtful. The chronological split, single test-set evaluation, preprocessing fit-on-train discipline, and explicit label leakage controls are all correct. I will focus on the genuine methodological issues rather than manufacture concerns.

**Issue 1: The labeling definition creates systematic label leakage via run-length selection bias**

The specific claim: Labeling a 'run' as failure-imminent if an unplanned work order falls within T_horizon days of when the run begins is a clean causal framing.

Why it can fail: A 'run' is defined as a continuous operation period. Runs that end in failure are structurally shorter — the pump fails and is taken offline — while normal runs may span months. This means that in practice, the label is partly a function of how long the run has already been operating at the point of labeling. A run that started one week before failure will almost certainly be labeled positive; a run that started six months before failure and is observed at day 1 would be labeled negative, even if the failure signal is already present. The model therefore partially learns 'run age at prediction time' rather than sensor degradation. This is not temporal leakage per se, but a selection artifact in the label construction that makes the problem easier than production deployment, where the model must decide early in a run whether failure is imminent within the next T_horizon days regardless of when the run started. The design does not describe when within a run the prediction is made — if it is always at the start of the run, the distribution of run ages at prediction time is identical between train and test, but the problem is still easier on short runs near failure than on long healthy runs observed early.

What would constitute evidence: Stratify performance by 'days since run start at prediction time.' If AUC degrades substantially for predictions made very early in a run (days 1–3), that confirms the model is exploiting run-length correlation with the label rather than genuine degradation signal.

**Issue 2: The comparison between Set A and Set B does not isolate temporal structure — it conflates temporal structure with feature richness**

The specific claim: The baseline (Set B) removes the temporal dimension while using the same W-hour window, so any performance difference is attributed to temporal structure.

Why it can fail: Set A includes rate-of-change features (derivatives, velocity, acceleration), rolling statistics over multiple time scales (1h, 8h, 24h windows), and the raw time series. Set B includes only single-window aggregates. The LSTM therefore receives strictly more information — not just temporally structured information, but more features. The correct ablation to isolate temporal structure would be: give the LSTM and the logistic regression the *identical* feature matrix (including all rolling stats and derivatives computed over all time scales in Set A), with the only difference being that the LSTM sees these as a sequence while logistic regression sees them as a flat vector. As designed, if the LSTM wins, it could be because rolling stats over 1h windows are informative and the baseline simply did not receive them — not because the LSTM's sequential processing adds value over a rich flat feature vector. The XGBoost secondary baseline partially addresses this only if XGBoost also receives Set A features, which the design does not specify (it says 'aggregated features').

What would constitute evidence: Add a third condition — XGBoost or logistic regression trained on the full Set A feature set (all rolling stats, derivatives, all time scales) flattened into a vector. If the LSTM still outperforms this, that is genuine evidence for sequential modeling. If not, the win is attributable to feature engineering, not architecture.

**Issue 3: ROC AUC as the primary hyperparameter selection criterion at 2–5% positive rate inflates apparent LSTM advantage**

The specific claim: ROC AUC on the validation set is an unbiased guide for model selection and correctly reflects operational value.

Why it can fail: At 2–5% positive rate, ROC AUC is dominated by the much larger negative class and can remain high even when precision at any operationally relevant threshold is near the base rate. More importantly, selecting the model that maximizes validation AUC, then separately choosing a threshold to hit 80% recall, decouples the metric used for selection from the metric used for deployment. A model that achieves AUC 0.82 with reasonable precision at 80% recall might be passed over in favor of one with AUC 0.85 that achieves that recall only by flooding the plant with false alarms. This is especially problematic for the LSTM, which has many more hyperparameters and can overfit to validation in ways that inflate AUC without genuine generalization. The precision reported on the test set is the operationally relevant quantity, but it is not what was optimized during selection.

What would constitute evidence: Report validation-set precision at the 80%-recall threshold for each candidate configuration during tuning, not just AUC. If the selected configuration's test-set precision degrades significantly relative to validation (>10 absolute points), that confirms the AUC-based selection criterion led to overfitting on the wrong objective. Comparing the AUC-selected model against the 'best precision at 80% recall on validation' model would directly test whether the selection criterion matters.

**Issue 4: 3–5 pump units is insufficient to distinguish temporal structure from unit-specific fixed effects**

The specific claim: Performance generalizes to future runs from the same plant, and the temporal split correctly simulates deployment.

Why it can fail: With 3–5 pump units and a chronological split, the test set contains the most recent runs from a small number of specific pumps. If one pump happened to develop a characteristic failure signature in the test period that was also present (at lower intensity) in the training period for that same pump, the LSTM could learn that pump's specific signature — a fixed effect — rather than a general temporal degradation pattern. The hold-out unit test in Section 8 partially addresses this, but it is listed as 'if feasible.' With 3 units, holding one out leaves only 2 for training, which may be insufficient to train a stable LSTM. The methodology does not specify how the temporal CV in Section 8 handles the unit dimension, so it may inadvertently allow unit-level fixed effects to inflate training-set CV estimates.

What would constitute evidence: The stratified performance by pump model (Section 9) is necessary but not sufficient — it tells you whether performance differs across units, not whether the LSTM learned unit-specific artifacts. The hold-out unit test (Section 8, item 4) is the correct test. If it shows substantially lower AUC than the full-data test, fixed-effect exploitation is likely. This should be elevated from 'if feasible' to required.

**Summary:** Issues 2 and 3 share the root cause that the experimental comparisons are not precisely controlled — the baseline comparison conflates feature richness with architectural advantage, and the selection criterion is misaligned with the deployment objective. Issues 1 and 4 share the root cause that with a small number of short-lived runs per unit, the label and training distribution are more unit-specific than the methodology acknowledges. None of these are fatal to the study as a whole, but Issue 2 is serious enough that the core hypothesis — temporal structure provides the advantage — could be falsely confirmed. The fix (add a flat-feature-rich baseline) is straightforward and should be required before drawing conclusions.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis of Each Critique Point

### Critique 1: Run-Length Selection Bias in Labeling

**Critic's claim:** Failure runs are structurally shorter, so 'run age at prediction time' is partially confounded with the label. The model may learn run-length correlates rather than sensor degradation. This makes the problem artificially easier on near-failure runs and inflates apparent performance.

**Assessment:** This is a valid and non-trivial observation. The labeling scheme — 'positive if an unplanned work order falls within T_horizon days of run start' — does create a structural asymmetry. A pump that fails after 8 days of a new run is clearly labeled positive. A pump that has been running for 6 months is labeled negative at day 1, even if it is already beginning to degrade. This is not temporal leakage in the classic sense (no future data enters features), but it is a selection artifact that makes the positive-class distribution systematically skewed toward shorter runs.

**Counter-argument:** The design does establish a fixed prediction horizon cutoff and excludes data after that cutoff. The window W=720h means the model sees the same fixed lookback regardless of run age. However, the critic is correct that run age at prediction time is not controlled. If predictions are always made at run start, then positive-labeled runs will on average have spent less time accumulating 'normal' baseline behavior before the prediction window, which could make the degradation signal more salient. The proposed diagnostic — stratify AUC by 'days since run start at prediction time' — is the right test and is not included in the current design.

**Concession:** This is a genuine methodological gap. The fix is tractable: explicitly define when within a run the prediction is made (e.g., at fixed intervals), and add the stratified analysis by run age. I concede this point partially — it is a real confound, but the severity depends on empirical measurement. It does not necessarily invalidate the study if addressed in analysis.

**Remaining uncertainty:** How large the run-age correlation with label actually is in practice depends on the distribution of run lengths in this specific plant. In plants with many short production cycles, this may be minor.

---

### Critique 2: Baseline Comparison Conflates Temporal Structure with Feature Richness

**Critic's claim:** Set A includes rolling statistics, derivatives, and raw time series that Set B does not. An LSTM win could reflect richer features rather than sequential modeling. The correct isolation requires giving the non-sequential baselines the identical feature set as the LSTM.

**Assessment:** This is the most serious critique and is substantially correct. The design intent was to compare 'sequence model' vs. 'aggregated features only,' treating feature richness as part of the architectural package. However, from a hypothesis-testing standpoint, the stated hypothesis is that 'temporal structure in sensor data provides genuine predictive signal.' If the LSTM wins because 1h rolling statistics (which are in Set A but not Set B) are informative, and logistic regression on those same rolling statistics would perform equally well, the hypothesis as stated is not actually confirmed — temporal structure would not be the source of gain.

**Counter-argument:** The XGBoost secondary baseline is intended to address model class differences, but only if it receives Set A features — which the methodology does not specify. As written, both baselines receive Set B. This is an unambiguous design gap. The critic's proposed fix is straightforward: add a third condition where LR or XGBoost receives the full Set A feature vector (all rolling stats, derivatives, all time scales) flattened into a vector. If the LSTM still outperforms this 'flat-but-rich' baseline, that is genuine evidence for sequential modeling. If the LSTM wins only over Set B, the win is attributable to feature engineering choices, not architecture.

**Concession:** I concede this point fully. This is a serious flaw in the experimental design as written. The core hypothesis cannot be confirmed without this third baseline condition. The fix is low-cost (train XGBoost on Set A features) and must be included before conclusions about temporal structure are drawn.

**Remaining uncertainty:** None — the fix is unambiguous and required.

---

### Critique 3: AUC-Based Hyperparameter Selection Misaligned with Deployment Objective

**Critic's claim:** Selecting configurations by validation AUC at 2–5% positive rate can favor models that rank well across the full ROC curve but perform poorly at the specific operating point (80% recall). This decouples selection from deployment and may allow LSTM overfitting via AUC inflation.

**Assessment:** This is a valid concern. ROC AUC at low prevalence is dominated by true negative rate performance, and a model can achieve strong AUC by being excellent at the easy part of the problem (ranking clearly normal runs near the bottom). Whether the selected model achieves good precision at the 80%-recall threshold is a separate question. The design specifies threshold selection on validation data, but does not require that configurations are compared on precision-at-80%-recall during tuning — only on AUC.

**Counter-argument:** AUC is a standard and defensible primary tuning metric because it is threshold-independent and less noisy than precision-at-a-fixed-recall on small validation sets. With only 2–5% positives in the validation set, precision-at-80%-recall may be highly variable across folds due to small positive counts, making it a noisier selection criterion. Using AUC for selection and then reporting precision on the held-out test set is a common and generally defensible protocol. The concern about 'flooding the plant with false alarms' is real, but is measured and reported at test time — if the selected model achieves poor precision at 80% recall on the test set, the methodology will correctly surface that.

**Partial concession:** The critic is right that the selection criterion and deployment metric are decoupled. The design should additionally report validation-set precision at the 80%-recall threshold for all candidate configurations, and optionally add precision-at-recall as a secondary selection criterion. However, I do not fully concede that this makes the design invalid — the decoupling is a known limitation and not a fatal flaw, provided test-set precision is reported (which it is). The risk of AUC-inflation-driven overfitting is real but manageable with the existing regularization and early stopping setup.

**Remaining uncertainty:** Whether the AUC-optimal configuration meaningfully differs from the precision-at-recall-optimal configuration in this specific dataset is an empirical question.

---

### Critique 4: 3–5 Pump Units Insufficient to Distinguish Temporal Degradation from Unit-Specific Fixed Effects

**Critic's claim:** With a chronological split on 3–5 units, the test set contains the most recent runs from specific pumps. The LSTM may learn unit-specific failure signatures (fixed effects) rather than generalizable temporal degradation patterns. The hold-out unit test should be required, not optional.

**Assessment:** This is a genuine limitation. With 3–5 pump units, the between-unit variance is large relative to the within-unit variance, and the chronological split preserves unit identity — meaning the LSTM sees early-period data from Unit A in training and is tested on later-period data from the same Unit A. If Unit A has a characteristic vibration signature before failure that is idiosyncratic to its mechanical configuration, the LSTM can learn that fixed effect.

**Counter-argument:** The stratified performance by pump model (Section 9) does provide some evidence on this: if performance is strong on all pump models, it is less likely to be purely driven by one unit's fixed effect. The attention weight analysis (Section 8, item 3) also helps identify whether the model relies on idiosyncratic versus general features. However, the critic is correct that these are necessary but insufficient — the hold-out unit test is the only direct test of generalization across units.

**Partial concession:** I concede that the hold-out unit test should be elevated from 'if feasible' to 'required if N ≥ 4 units.' The concern about training instability with only 2 units remaining is real for N=3, but with N=4–5, a leave-one-unit-out test is feasible and should be required. I also concede that the temporal CV in Section 8 should explicitly stratify by unit to avoid unit-level leakage in CV estimates. However, I note that the concern is partially mitigated by the stratified performance reporting, which will at minimum reveal whether any unit is an extreme outlier.

**Remaining uncertainty:** How much of the expected performance is driven by unit-specific vs. general patterns depends on the mechanical diversity of the pump fleet, which is plant-specific.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Reviewing the concessions:
- Issue 1 (run-length selection bias): Partially conceded — real confound, tractable fix, severity is empirical.
- Issue 2 (feature richness vs. temporal structure): Fully conceded — serious flaw, the core hypothesis cannot be confirmed as designed without the flat-but-rich baseline.
- Issue 3 (AUC selection criterion): Partially conceded — real decoupling, manageable limitation, not fatal.
- Issue 4 (unit-specific fixed effects): Partially conceded — hold-out unit test should be required for N≥4, not optional.

Issue 2 is a full concession on the core hypothesis test. The study as designed cannot distinguish 'temporal structure helps' from 'richer features help' — which is the central question. Issues 1 and 4 add further uncertainty about what exactly the model is learning. Issue 3 introduces selection-criterion misalignment that could compound these problems. Together, these represent multiple genuine flaws that cannot be dismissed as minor.

However, all four issues have clear, low-cost fixes: (1) add run-age stratification in analysis; (2) add flat-but-rich baseline; (3) track precision-at-recall during tuning; (4) elevate hold-out unit test to required. These are design amendments, not fundamental reconceptions. The methodology's core infrastructure (chronological split, preprocessing discipline, single test evaluation, label leakage controls) is sound.

The verdict is `empirical_test_agreed` rather than `critique_wins` because the fixes do not require the study to be abandoned — they require the study to be run with the corrected design, and the outcome remains genuinely uncertain. The critic has correctly identified what the study needs to demonstrate; the study does not yet demonstrate it as designed.
all_issues_raised: Run-length selection bias in labeling: failure runs are structurally shorter, so run age at prediction time is partially confounded with the label, making early-run predictions artificially harder and inflating apparent model performance on near-failure runs.

Baseline comparison conflates temporal structure with feature richness: Set B lacks rolling statistics and derivatives that Set A provides, so LSTM wins could reflect richer features rather than sequential modeling — a flat-feature-rich baseline (XGBoost or LR on Set A features) is missing.
AUC-based hyperparameter selection is misaligned with the precision-at-80%-recall deployment objective, risking selection of models that inflate AUC at the cost of precision under class imbalance.

With only 3–5 pump units, the chronological split cannot reliably distinguish temporal degradation patterns from unit-specific fixed effects; the hold-out unit test is listed as optional but is required to validate the core generalization claim.
all_issues_adjudicated: Run-length selection bias in labeling survives: failure runs are structurally shorter, making run age at prediction time a partial confound with the label; the design lacks the stratified-by-run-age analysis needed to measure or bound this effect, and the defender conceded the gap while noting severity is empirically uncertain.

Baseline comparison conflates temporal structure with feature richness and survives: Set B lacks rolling statistics and derivatives present in Set A, so an LSTM win cannot be attributed to sequential modeling without a flat-but-rich baseline (XGBoost or LR on full Set A features); the defender fully conceded this as a serious flaw that prevents confirming the core hypothesis as designed.

AUC-based hyperparameter selection misaligned with precision-at-80%-recall deployment objective survives: the selection criterion is decoupled from the operationally relevant metric, which could favor models that inflate AUC at the cost of precision under class imbalance; the defender partially conceded and acknowledged the empirical question of whether the AUC-optimal and precision-at-recall-optimal configurations actually differ remains open.

Insufficient pump units to distinguish temporal degradation from unit-specific fixed effects survives: with 3–5 units the hold-out unit test is the only direct test of cross-unit generalization, yet it is listed as optional; the defender conceded it should be required for N≥4 units, leaving the generalization claim unvalidated as currently designed.
